sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A lab prepares a phosphate buffer using H2PO4− and HPO4²−, with pKa = 7.20 for the relevant acid-base pair. Four solutions are made with [base]/[acid] ratios of 0.10, 0.50, 1.0, and 10.0. Measured pH values are approximately 6.20, 6.90, 7.20, and 8.20. Students use the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to evaluate which mixture best resists pH change near physiological pH.
questions.stem: At what base/acid ratio does pH equal pKa?

explanations.C: Correct. Henderson-Hasselbalch gives pH = pKa + log([base]/[acid]). When the ratio is 1, log(1)=0, so pH = pKa.
explanations.A: A ratio of 0.10 gives pH one unit below pKa.
explanations.B: A ratio of 0.50 gives pH below pKa.
explanations.D: A ratio of 10 gives pH one unit above pKa.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. pH values for phosphate buffers with different conjugate base-to-acid ratios.

explanations.B: Correct. pH = 7.20 + log(10) = 8.20. This also matches the plotted value at ratio 10.
explanations.D: 10.0 is the ratio, not the pH.

questions.stem: Which mixture should have greatest buffering capacity near pH 7.2?

explanations.D: Correct. Buffers are most effective near pH = pKa, when acid and conjugate base amounts are similar. The 1.0 ratio mixture is centered at pH 7.20.
explanations.A: The 0.10 mixture is one pH unit below pKa and has less balanced components.
explanations.B: The 10.0 mixture is one pH unit above pKa and is not centered at pH 7.2.
explanations.C: Pure water lacks appreciable conjugate acid-base buffering capacity.

questions.stem: Adding a small amount of strong acid to the buffer primarily consumes which species?

explanations.A: Correct. Added H+ is neutralized by the conjugate base HPO4²− to form H2PO4−. This is how the buffer resists a sharp pH drop.
explanations.B: H2PO4− is the acid form; it would react with added base more directly.
explanations.D: Water participates in acid-base chemistry but is not the main buffer component consuming added acid.

questions.stem: Why does the pH change by one unit when the base/acid ratio changes from 1 to 10?

choices.D: The logarithm of the ratio changes by 1

explanations.D: Correct. Henderson-Hasselbalch is logarithmic. Increasing [base]/[acid] from 1 to 10 changes log ratio from 0 to 1, raising pH by exactly one unit if pKa is fixed.
explanations.A: pKa is a property of the acid-base pair and does not change with ratio.
explanations.B: A ratio of 10 does not mean acid concentration is zero.
explanations.C: The solution can still buffer, though its optimal range shifts.

questions.passage: Students study the solubility of AgCl(s) in water and in solutions containing chloride. The solubility product is Ksp = 1.8 × 10−10. In pure water, the molar solubility is approximately 1.3 × 10−5 M. In 0.010 M NaCl, the molar solubility falls to about 1.8 × 10−8 M. The class uses the common-ion effect to explain why added chloride suppresses dissolution.
questions.stem: What happens to AgCl solubility when 0.010 M NaCl is added?

explanations.C: Correct. Added chloride is a common ion and shifts AgCl dissolution equilibrium toward solid AgCl. The graph shows solubility dropping from 1.3×10⁻5 M to 1.8×10⁻8 M.
explanations.A: The common-ion effect suppresses, rather than increases, solubility.
explanations.B: The figure shows a large change.
explanations.D: 0.010 M is the added chloride concentration, not the AgCl solubility.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Molar solubility of AgCl in pure water and in 0.010 M NaCl solution.

questions.stem: What is the expression for Ksp of AgCl?

explanations.B: Correct. For AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag+ + Cl−, the solid is omitted from the equilibrium expression, so Ksp = [Ag+][Cl−].
explanations.A: Pure solids are omitted from K expressions.
explanations.C: The ions are multiplied, not divided.
explanations.D: The solid does not appear in the numerator.

questions.stem: Estimate AgCl solubility in 0.010 M Cl− using Ksp.

explanations.A: Correct. With [Cl−] ≈ 0.010 M, [Ag+] = Ksp/[Cl−] = 1.8×10⁻10/1.0×10⁻2 = 1.8×10⁻8 M. This equals the molar solubility.
explanations.B: This value is too high by a factor of 100.
explanations.C: 1.3×10⁻5 M is the pure-water solubility, not the common-ion result.
explanations.D: This value divides by the wrong power of ten.

questions.stem: Why is Cl− called a common ion in this experiment?

choices.C: It is already a product of AgCl dissolution and is added from NaCl
choices.A: It is present only in the solid phase
choices.B: It is consumed by water to make oxygen
choices.D: It changes Ksp to a larger value

explanations.C: Correct. Chloride is produced when AgCl dissolves and is also supplied by NaCl. Adding a product shifts equilibrium back toward the solid.
explanations.A: Chloride is present in solution when NaCl dissolves.
explanations.B: Water does not consume chloride to make oxygen in this equilibrium.
explanations.D: Ksp remains fixed at a given temperature; Q changes when chloride is added.

questions.stem: If enough AgNO3 were added to a chloride solution, what would likely form?

explanations.B: Correct. Adding Ag+ raises Q = [Ag+][Cl−]. If Q exceeds Ksp, AgCl(s) precipitates.
explanations.A: NaNO3 is generally soluble and is not the precipitate predicted by AgCl Ksp.
explanations.C: This precipitation reaction does not generate oxygen gas.
explanations.D: Adding silver ions tends to remove chloride as AgCl, not create only more dissolved chloride.
